content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:0e4e4c46

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: force_memory_write=true and transcript includes extractable durable preferences/experiences plus richer game-night recommendation context.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements require resolving to the stable User entity; no existing candidates provided.
- mention=game night; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=CONCEPT; name=Game night; reason=Reusable event concept discussed as something the user hosts.
- mention=Ticket to Ride; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PRODUCT; name=Ticket to Ride; reason=Concrete board game repeatedly discussed and likely needed for later claims.
- mention=Settlers of Catan; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=PRODUCT; name=Settlers of Catan; reason=Concrete board game recommended in the transcript.
- mention=Carcassonne; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=PRODUCT; name=Carcassonne; reason=Concrete board game discussed and selected for smaller groups.
- mention=El Grande; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=PRODUCT; name=El Grande; reason=Concrete board game recommended in the transcript.
- mention=7 Wonders; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=PRODUCT; name=7 Wonders; reason=Concrete board game recommended in the transcript.
- mention=Pandemic; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=PRODUCT; name=Pandemic; reason=Concrete board game recommended in the transcript.
- mention=Azul; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=PRODUCT; name=Azul; reason=Concrete board game recommended in the transcript.
- mention=Splendor; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=PRODUCT; name=Splendor; reason=Concrete board game recommended in the transcript.
- mention=Elfenland; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=PRODUCT; name=Elfenland; reason=Concrete board game recommended in the transcript.
- mention=Russian Railroads; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=PRODUCT; name=Russian Railroads; reason=Concrete board game recommended in the transcript.
- mention=Trains; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<12>; type=PRODUCT; name=Trains; reason=Concrete board game recommended in the transcript.
- mention=Wits & Wagers; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<13>; type=PRODUCT; name=Wits & Wagers; reason=Concrete party game recommended for larger groups.
- mention=Codenames; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<14>; type=PRODUCT; name=Codenames; reason=Concrete game the user has played and liked.
- mention=Taboo; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<15>; type=PRODUCT; name=Taboo; reason=Concrete party game recommended for larger groups.
- mention=Deception: Murder in Hong Kong; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<16>; type=PRODUCT; name=Deception: Murder in Hong Kong; reason=Concrete game the user is considering and later chooses.
- mention=Two Rooms and a Boom; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<17>; type=PRODUCT; name=Two Rooms and a Boom; reason=Concrete game the user is considering.
- mention=The Resistance; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<18>; type=PRODUCT; name=The Resistance; reason=Concrete party game recommended for larger groups.
- mention=Monikers; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<19>; type=PRODUCT; name=Monikers; reason=Concrete party game recommended for larger groups.
- mention=Junk Art; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<20>; type=PRODUCT; name=Junk Art; reason=Concrete party game recommended for larger groups.
- mention=Spyfall; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<21>; type=PRODUCT; name=Spyfall; reason=Concrete party game recommended for larger groups.
- mention=What Do You Meme; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<22>; type=PRODUCT; name=What Do You Meme; reason=Concrete game the user has played and liked.
- mention=my friends; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<23>; type=CONCEPT; name=User's friends; reason=Reusable social group appears as participants in the user's game nights and liked games.
- mention=new gamers; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<24>; type=CONCEPT; name=New gamers; reason=Concrete participant category relevant to later claims about inclusive game nights.
- mention=experienced gamers; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<25>; type=CONCEPT; name=Experienced gamers; reason=Concrete participant category relevant to later claims about inclusive game nights.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<26>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/25 (Thu) 19:52
Transcript:
user: I'm thinking of hosting another game night soon and I'd like some recommendations for games that are similar to Ticket to Ride. I've been really enjoying the strategy aspect of it.
assistant: Ticket to Ride is an excellent game! If you enjoy its strategic gameplay, here are some game recommendations that you might enjoy:

1. **Settlers of Catan**: A classic game of resource management, trading, and building. Players collect resources to build roads, settlements, and cities on an island. Like Ticket to Ride, it requires strategic planning and adaptability.
2. **Carcassonne**: A tile-laying game where players build a medieval landscape, placing tiles to create cities, roads, and fields. It shares similarities with Ticket to Ride's route-building mechanics and requires strategic planning.
3. **El Grande**: A game of area control and strategy, where players build territories, collect victory points, and manage resources. It has a similar feel to Ticket to Ride's train network building.
4. **7 Wonders**: A card-drafting game where players build up their civilization by collecting cards representing different structures, technologies, and military units. Like Ticket to Ride, it requires strategic planning and resource management.
5. **Pandemic**: Work together with friends to stop the spread of diseases and save the world! This cooperative game shares similarities with Ticket to Ride's resource management and strategic planning.
6. **Azul**: A tile-laying game where players compete to create the most beautiful mosaic. It requires strategic planning and resource management, similar to Ticket to Ride's train car management.
7. **Splendor**: A card-collecting game where players build up their deck to collect victory points. It has a similar feel to Ticket to Ride's train car collection and strategic planning.
8. **Elfenland**: A route-building game where players build roads and collect victory points by connecting cities. It shares similarities with Ticket to Ride's train network building.
9. **Russian Railroads**: A train-themed game where players build railroad tracks, manage resources, and collect victory points. It's a more complex game than Ticket to Ride, but shares similar mechanics.
10. **Trains**: A deck-building game where players build train routes and collect victory points. It has a similar feel to Ticket to Ride's train car collection and strategic planning.

These games offer varying levels of complexity and gameplay, but all share elements of strategy and resource management that make Ticket to Ride so enjoyable. Have fun exploring these options and finding your next favorite game!
user: I'm interested in trying out Carcassonne, I've heard great things about it. Do you think it's a good game for a group of 6-8 people, or is it better suited for smaller groups? Also, by the way, I've been crushing it in Ticket to Ride lately - my highest score so far is 124 points, and I'm eager to keep improving.
assistant: Congratulations on your Ticket to Ride victories! 124 points is an impressive score!

Regarding Carcassonne, it's a great choice! While it can be played with 2-5 players, it's generally recommended for 2-4 players. The gameplay and scoring mechanics are designed for a smaller group, and with 6-8 players, the game may become too chaotic and slow-paced.

Here's why:

1. **Tile draw and placement**: With more players, the tile draw and placement process can take longer, leading to downtime between turns. Each player needs to carefully consider their tile placement, and with more players, this can lead to analysis paralysis.
2. **Farm scoring**: In Carcassonne, players score points for completed features like cities, roads, and monasteries. With more players, the number of features on the board increases, making it harder to keep track of scoring and leading to more disputes.
3. **Limited interaction**: Carcassonne is a relatively low-interaction game, and with more players, the opportunities for meaningful interactions and strategic planning decrease.

That being said, if you still want to play with 6-8 people, you can consider the following:

1. **Use the "Team Play" variant**: Divide the group into teams of 2-3 players each. This way, each team works together to build their own landscape, and scoring is combined.
2. **Use multiple boards**: Set up multiple boards, each with 2-4 players. This way, each group can play their own game, and you can have a mini-tournament to see which group scores the highest.

However, if you have a smaller group of 2-4 players, Carcassonne is an excellent choice. It's a great introduction to tile-laying games, and its simple yet strategic gameplay makes it appealing to new and experienced gamers alike.

Keep crushing it in Ticket to Ride, and I hope you enjoy your Carcassonne experience!
user: I think I'll stick to playing Carcassonne with a smaller group then. What are some other games that are suitable for a larger group of 6-8 people?
assistant: There are many games that shine with larger groups. Here are some excellent options for 6-8 players:

1. **Wits & Wagers**: A party game where players bet on the answers given by their opponents to trivia questions. It's a hilarious and social game that's perfect for larger groups.
2. **Codenames**: A word-guessing game where players try to identify words based on one-word clues given by their teammates. It's a great game for larger groups, and there's even a "Party Edition" designed for 6-12 players!
3. **Taboo**: A classic word-describing game where players take turns explaining a word without using certain "taboo" words or phrases. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's easy to learn.
4. **Deception: Murder in Hong Kong**: A social deduction game where one player takes on the role of the investigator, and the others play as suspects. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's a lot of fun.
5. **Two Rooms and a Boom**: A social deduction game where players are divided into two teams, and they need to figure out who's on their team and who's not. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's easy to learn.
6. **The Resistance**: A popular social deduction game where players work together to complete missions, but some players are spies trying to sabotage the efforts. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's a lot of fun.
7. **Monikers**: A party game where players try to guess the name of a movie, book, or other item based on a brief description given by the player who drew it. It's a hilarious game that's perfect for larger groups.
8. **Junk Art**: A dexterity game where players compete to create the most impressive art pieces using random junk. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's a lot of fun.
9. **Spyfall**: A social deduction game where players ask questions to figure out who among them is a spy. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's easy to learn.
10. **What Do You Meme**: A party game where players compete to create the funniest meme by combining caption cards with a random image. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's a lot of fun.

These games are all highly rated and suitable for larger groups. Keep in mind that some games may have longer playtimes or more complex rules, so make sure to read the instructions and explain the rules clearly to your group.

I hope this helps, and I'm sure you'll have a blast with your group!
user: I've already played Codenames and What Do You Meme with my friends, and we all loved them. I'm considering getting Deception: Murder in Hong Kong or Two Rooms and a Boom for my next game night. Do you think they're similar to each other, or do they offer different experiences?
assistant: Great choices! Both Deception: Murder in Hong Kong and Two Rooms and a Boom are social deduction games, but they offer distinct experiences. Here's a brief comparison to help you decide:

**Similarities:**

* Both games are social deduction games, where players need to figure out who's on their team and who's not.
* Both games involve hidden roles and bluffing.
* Both games are great for larger groups (6-8 players).

**Differences:**

* **Theme and Story:** Deception: Murder in Hong Kong is a murder mystery game where one player takes on the role of the investigator, and the others play as suspects. Two Rooms and a Boom is a more abstract game where players are divided into two teams, with some players being "bombs" that need to be eliminated.
* **Gameplay:** In Deception, the investigator asks questions, and suspects respond with clues or lies. The investigator must figure out who's telling the truth and who's hiding something. In Two Rooms and a Boom, players are divided into two rooms and exchange cards with players in the other room. Players need to figure out who's on their team and who's a bomb.
* **Complexity:** Deception: Murder in Hong Kong has more complex rules and requires more strategic thinking, as the investigator needs to piece together clues and make accusations. Two Rooms and a Boom is more straightforward, with simpler rules and a faster gameplay pace.
* **Interactivity:** Both games involve player interaction, but Deception: Murder in Hong Kong is more focused on individual interactions between the investigator and suspects. Two Rooms and a Boom involves more group discussion and negotiation between players in each room.
* **Tone:** Deception: Murder in Hong Kong has a more serious, detective-like tone, while Two Rooms and a Boom is more light-hearted and chaotic.

Consider the following questions to help you decide between the two games:

* Do you prefer a more complex, strategic game with a stronger theme (Deception: Murder in Hong Kong)? Or a faster-paced, more chaotic game with simpler rules (Two Rooms and a Boom)?
* Do you enjoy playing as an investigator, gathering clues, and making accusations (Deception)? Or do you prefer a more group-focused game with lots of discussion and negotiation (Two Rooms and a Boom)?
* Is your group more interested in a murder mystery theme or a more abstract, action-packed game?

Ultimately, both games are excellent choices, and you can't go wrong with either one. If you're looking for a more complex, strategic game with a strong theme, Deception: Murder in Hong Kong might be the better choice. If you prefer a faster-paced, more chaotic game with simpler rules, Two Rooms and a Boom could be the way to go.
user: I think I'll go with Deception: Murder in Hong Kong. I like the idea of playing as an investigator and gathering clues to solve the mystery. Plus, the theme seems really engaging and immersive.
assistant: Deception: Murder in Hong Kong is an excellent game that offers a unique and engaging experience. The theme and gameplay mechanics work together to create a thrilling and immersive atmosphere, and playing as the investigator can be a lot of fun.

As the investigator, you'll need to ask the right questions, analyze the clues, and make accusations to uncover the truth. It's a challenging but rewarding role, and it requires a combination of deduction, strategy, and social manipulation.

Here are some tips to help you get the most out of your Deception: Murder in Hong Kong experience:

1. **Pay attention to details**: As the investigator, you'll need to carefully examine the clues and statements from the suspects. Look for inconsistencies, contradictions, and suspicious behavior.
2. **Ask the right questions**: Craft your questions carefully to gather the most information. You can ask open-ended questions, yes/no questions, or even ask suspects to reveal specific information.
3. **Read people's behavior**: Observe the suspects' reactions, body language, and tone. This can give you valuable insights into their guilt or innocence.
4. **Keep track of evidence**: Organize the clues and evidence you've gathered to help you piece together the mystery.
5. **Make strategic accusations**: Timing is crucial when making accusations. Make sure you have enough evidence to support your claims, and try to catch the suspects off guard.
6. **Don't trust anyone**: Remember, some suspects might be lying, and others might be hiding information. Stay vigilant and keep an open mind.

As you play Deception: Murder in Hong Kong, you'll discover that it's not just about solving the mystery, but also about the social dynamics and interactions between players. The game encourages players to get into character, and the suspects will try to deceive and mislead you.

Have fun, and good luck solving the murder mystery!
user: I'm thinking of hosting a game night with a mix of new and experienced gamers. Do you have any tips on how to create a welcoming and inclusive environment for all players?
assistant: What a great question! Creating a welcoming and inclusive environment is crucial for a successful and enjoyable game night, especially when you have a mix of new and experienced gamers. Here are some tips to help you achieve that:

1. **Clear explanations**: Make sure to explain the rules and objectives of each game clearly, slowly, and patiently. Use simple language and provide examples to help new players understand. Encourage questions and clarify any doubts.
2. **Start with beginner-friendly games**: Choose games that are easy to learn and play, especially for new gamers. This will help them feel comfortable and build confidence. You can always introduce more complex games later in the night.
3. **Provide a comfortable space**: Ensure the playing area is comfortable, well-lit, and free from distractions. Make sure there's enough seating, tables, and snacks to go around.
4. **Encourage teamwork**: Pair new gamers with experienced players to create a buddy system. This helps new players learn from others and builds a sense of camaraderie.
5. **Foster a positive atmosphere**: Promote a friendly, relaxed, and supportive environment. Encourage players to have fun, laugh, and not take the games too seriously.
6. **Be patient and encouraging**: As the host, be patient with new players and offer guidance when needed. Praise their efforts and accomplishments, even if they make mistakes.
7. **Minimize intimidation**: Avoid using gaming jargon or technical terms that might confuse new players. Also, try to avoid dominating the game or being overly competitive, as this can intimidate others.
8. **Offer options and choices**: Provide a variety of games and let players choose which ones they want to play. This will cater to different interests and preferences.
9. **Rotate games and players**: Rotate games and players every hour or so to keep things fresh and exciting. This also gives new players a chance to try different games and meet new people.
10. **Be flexible**: Be prepared to adjust your plans or game choices based on the group's preferences and feedback. Be open to suggestions and willing to make changes to ensure everyone has a great time.

By following these tips, you'll create a welcoming and inclusive environment that makes all players feel comfortable, engaged, and excited to play. Remember, the goal is to have fun and socialize, so prioritize that over winning or being competitive.

Good luck with your game night, and I hope everyone has an amazing time!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm thinking of hosting another game night soon and I'd like some recommendations for games that are similar to Ticket to Ride. I've been really enjoying the strategy aspect of it.
assistant: Ticket to Ride is an excellent game! If you enjoy its strategic gameplay, here are some game recommendations that you might enjoy:

1. **Settlers of Catan**: A classic game of resource management, trading, and building. Players collect resources to build roads, settlements, and cities on an island. Like Ticket to Ride, it requires strategic planning and adaptability.
2. **Carcassonne**: A tile-laying game where players build a medieval landscape, placing tiles to create cities, roads, and fields. It shares similarities with Ticket to Ride's route-building mechanics and requires strategic planning.
3. **El Grande**: A game of area control and strategy, where players build territories, collect victory points, and manage resources. It has a similar feel to Ticket to Ride's train network building.
4. **7 Wonders**: A card-drafting game where players build up their civilization by collecting cards representing different structures, technologies, and military units. Like Ticket to Ride, it requires strategic planning and resource management.
5. **Pandemic**: Work together with friends to stop the spread of diseases and save the world! This cooperative game shares similarities with Ticket to Ride's resource management and strategic planning.
6. **Azul**: A tile-laying game where players compete to create the most beautiful mosaic. It requires strategic planning and resource management, similar to Ticket to Ride's train car management.
7. **Splendor**: A card-collecting game where players build up their deck to collect victory points. It has a similar feel to Ticket to Ride's train car collection and strategic planning.
8. **Elfenland**: A route-building game where players build roads and collect victory points by connecting cities. It shares similarities with Ticket to Ride's train network building.
9. **Russian Railroads**: A train-themed game where players build railroad tracks, manage resources, and collect victory points. It's a more complex game than Ticket to Ride, but shares similar mechanics.
10. **Trains**: A deck-building game where players build train routes and collect victory points. It has a similar feel to Ticket to Ride's train car collection and strategic planning.

These games offer varying levels of complexity and gameplay, but all share elements of strategy and resource management that make Ticket to Ride so enjoyable. Have fun exploring these options and finding your next favorite game!
user: I'm interested in trying out Carcassonne, I've heard great things about it. Do you think it's a good game for a group of 6-8 people, or is it better suited for smaller groups? Also, by the way, I've been crushing it in Ticket to Ride lately - my highest score so far is 124 points, and I'm eager to keep improving.
assistant: Congratulations on your Ticket to Ride victories! 124 points is an impressive score!

Regarding Carcassonne, it's a great choice! While it can be played with 2-5 players, it's generally recommended for 2-4 players. The gameplay and scoring mechanics are designed for a smaller group, and with 6-8 players, the game may become too chaotic and slow-paced.

Here's why:

1. **Tile draw and placement**: With more players, the tile draw and placement process can take longer, leading to downtime between turns. Each player needs to carefully consider their tile placement, and with more players, this can lead to analysis paralysis.
2. **Farm scoring**: In Carcassonne, players score points for completed features like cities, roads, and monasteries. With more players, the number of features on the board increases, making it harder to keep track of scoring and leading to more disputes.
3. **Limited interaction**: Carcassonne is a relatively low-interaction game, and with more players, the opportunities for meaningful interactions and strategic planning decrease.

That being said, if you still want to play with 6-8 people, you can consider the following:

1. **Use the "Team Play" variant**: Divide the group into teams of 2-3 players each. This way, each team works together to build their own landscape, and scoring is combined.
2. **Use multiple boards**: Set up multiple boards, each with 2-4 players. This way, each group can play their own game, and you can have a mini-tournament to see which group scores the highest.

However, if you have a smaller group of 2-4 players, Carcassonne is an excellent choice. It's a great introduction to tile-laying games, and its simple yet strategic gameplay makes it appealing to new and experienced gamers alike.

Keep crushing it in Ticket to Ride, and I hope you enjoy your Carcassonne experience!
user: I think I'll stick to playing Carcassonne with a smaller group then. What are some other games that are suitable for a larger group of 6-8 people?
assistant: There are many games that shine with larger groups. Here are some excellent options for 6-8 players:

1. **Wits & Wagers**: A party game where players bet on the answers given by their opponents to trivia questions. It's a hilarious and social game that's perfect for larger groups.
2. **Codenames**: A word-guessing game where players try to identify words based on one-word clues given by their teammates. It's a great game for larger groups, and there's even a "Party Edition" designed for 6-12 players!
3. **Taboo**: A classic word-describing game where players take turns explaining a word without using certain "taboo" words or phrases. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's easy to learn.
4. **Deception: Murder in Hong Kong**: A social deduction game where one player takes on the role of the investigator, and the others play as suspects. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's a lot of fun.
5. **Two Rooms and a Boom**: A social deduction game where players are divided into two teams, and they need to figure out who's on their team and who's not. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's easy to learn.
6. **The Resistance**: A popular social deduction game where players work together to complete missions, but some players are spies trying to sabotage the efforts. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's a lot of fun.
7. **Monikers**: A party game where players try to guess the name of a movie, book, or other item based on a brief description given by the player who drew it. It's a hilarious game that's perfect for larger groups.
8. **Junk Art**: A dexterity game where players compete to create the most impressive art pieces using random junk. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's a lot of fun.
9. **Spyfall**: A social deduction game where players ask questions to figure out who among them is a spy. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's easy to learn.
10. **What Do You Meme**: A party game where players compete to create the funniest meme by combining caption cards with a random image. It's a great game for larger groups, and it's a lot of fun.

These games are all highly rated and suitable for larger groups. Keep in mind that some games may have longer playtimes or more complex rules, so make sure to read the instructions and explain the rules clearly to your group.

I hope this helps, and I'm sure you'll have a blast with your group!
user: I've already played Codenames and What Do You Meme with my friends, and we all loved them. I'm considering getting Deception: Murder in Hong Kong or Two Rooms and a Boom for my next game night. Do you think they're similar to each other, or do they offer different experiences?
assistant: Great choices! Both Deception: Murder in Hong Kong and Two Rooms and a Boom are social deduction games, but they offer distinct experiences. Here's a brief comparison to help you decide:

**Similarities:**

* Both games are social deduction games, where players need to figure out who's on their team and who's not.
* Both games involve hidden roles and bluffing.
* Both games are great for larger groups (6-8 players).

**Differences:**

* **Theme and Story:** Deception: Murder in Hong Kong is a murder mystery game where one player takes on the role of the investigator, and the others play as suspects. Two Rooms and a Boom is a more abstract game where players are divided into two teams, with some players being "bombs" that need to be eliminated.
* **Gameplay:** In Deception, the investigator asks questions, and suspects respond with clues or lies. The investigator must figure out who's telling the truth and who's hiding something. In Two Rooms and a Boom, players are divided into two rooms and exchange cards with players in the other room. Players need to figure out who's on their team and who's a bomb.
* **Complexity:** Deception: Murder in Hong Kong has more complex rules and requires more strategic thinking, as the investigator needs to piece together clues and make accusations. Two Rooms and a Boom is more straightforward, with simpler rules and a faster gameplay pace.
* **Interactivity:** Both games involve player interaction, but Deception: Murder in Hong Kong is more focused on individual interactions between the investigator and suspects. Two Rooms and a Boom involves more group discussion and negotiation between players in each room.
* **Tone:** Deception: Murder in Hong Kong has a more serious, detective-like tone, while Two Rooms and a Boom is more light-hearted and chaotic.

Consider the following questions to help you decide between the two games:

* Do you prefer a more complex, strategic game with a stronger theme (Deception: Murder in Hong Kong)? Or a faster-paced, more chaotic game with simpler rules (Two Rooms and a Boom)?
* Do you enjoy playing as an investigator, gathering clues, and making accusations (Deception)? Or do you prefer a more group-focused game with lots of discussion and negotiation (Two Rooms and a Boom)?
* Is your group more interested in a murder mystery theme or a more abstract, action-packed game?

Ultimately, both games are excellent choices, and you can't go wrong with either one. If you're looking for a more complex, strategic game with a strong theme, Deception: Murder in Hong Kong might be the better choice. If you prefer a faster-paced, more chaotic game with simpler rules, Two Rooms and a Boom could be the way to go.
user: I think I'll go with Deception: Murder in Hong Kong. I like the idea of playing as an investigator and gathering clues to solve the mystery. Plus, the theme seems really engaging and immersive.
assistant: Deception: Murder in Hong Kong is an excellent game that offers a unique and engaging experience. The theme and gameplay mechanics work together to create a thrilling and immersive atmosphere, and playing as the investigator can be a lot of fun.

As the investigator, you'll need to ask the right questions, analyze the clues, and make accusations to uncover the truth. It's a challenging but rewarding role, and it requires a combination of deduction, strategy, and social manipulation.

Here are some tips to help you get the most out of your Deception: Murder in Hong Kong experience:

1. **Pay attention to details**: As the investigator, you'll need to carefully examine the clues and statements from the suspects. Look for inconsistencies, contradictions, and suspicious behavior.
2. **Ask the right questions**: Craft your questions carefully to gather the most information. You can ask open-ended questions, yes/no questions, or even ask suspects to reveal specific information.
3. **Read people's behavior**: Observe the suspects' reactions, body language, and tone. This can give you valuable insights into their guilt or innocence.
4. **Keep track of evidence**: Organize the clues and evidence you've gathered to help you piece together the mystery.
5. **Make strategic accusations**: Timing is crucial when making accusations. Make sure you have enough evidence to support your claims, and try to catch the suspects off guard.
6. **Don't trust anyone**: Remember, some suspects might be lying, and others might be hiding information. Stay vigilant and keep an open mind.

As you play Deception: Murder in Hong Kong, you'll discover that it's not just about solving the mystery, but also about the social dynamics and interactions between players. The game encourages players to get into character, and the suspects will try to deceive and mislead you.

Have fun, and good luck solving the murder mystery!
user: I'm thinking of hosting a game night with a mix of new and experienced gamers. Do you have any tips on how to create a welcoming and inclusive environment for all players?
assistant: What a great question! Creating a welcoming and inclusive environment is crucial for a successful and enjoyable game night, especially when you have a mix of new and experienced gamers. Here are some tips to help you achieve that:

1. **Clear explanations**: Make sure to explain the rules and objectives of each game clearly, slowly, and patiently. Use simple language and provide examples to help new players understand. Encourage questions and clarify any doubts.
2. **Start with beginner-friendly games**: Choose games that are easy to learn and play, especially for new gamers. This will help them feel comfortable and build confidence. You can always introduce more complex games later in the night.
3. **Provide a comfortable space**: Ensure the playing area is comfortable, well-lit, and free from distractions. Make sure there's enough seating, tables, and snacks to go around.
4. **Encourage teamwork**: Pair new gamers with experienced players to create a buddy system. This helps new players learn from others and builds a sense of camaraderie.
5. **Foster a positive atmosphere**: Promote a friendly, relaxed, and supportive environment. Encourage players to have fun, laugh, and not take the games too seriously.
6. **Be patient and encouraging**: As the host, be patient with new players and offer guidance when needed. Praise their efforts and accomplishments, even if they make mistakes.
7. **Minimize intimidation**: Avoid using gaming jargon or technical terms that might confuse new players. Also, try to avoid dominating the game or being overly competitive, as this can intimidate others.
8. **Offer options and choices**: Provide a variety of games and let players choose which ones they want to play. This will cater to different interests and preferences.
9. **Rotate games and players**: Rotate games and players every hour or so to keep things fresh and exciting. This also gives new players a chance to try different games and meet new people.
10. **Be flexible**: Be prepared to adjust your plans or game choices based on the group's preferences and feedback. Be open to suggestions and willing to make changes to ensure everyone has a great time.

By following these tips, you'll create a welcoming and inclusive environment that makes all players feel comfortable, engaged, and excited to play. Remember, the goal is to have fun and socialize, so prioritize that over winning or being competitive.

Good luck with your game night, and I hope everyone has an amazing time!
